sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 30-year-old female, presents with a history of asthma symptoms that have been persistent and frustrating, causing her to frequently visit urgent care and her primary care physician.
She reports that her asthma symptoms typically worsen when she gets sick with a cold, and she has noticed that exposure to her aunt's cat triggers her symptoms.
She uses a blue inhaler, which she believes is albuterol, and reports that it helps her symptoms but only after using two puffs.
She has had several emergency department visits and urgent care visits in the past year, including a recent exacerbation about a month ago.
She has not smoked or used tobacco products, but occasionally goes to bars where smoking irritates her.
She enjoys outdoor activities like hiking and has no history of seasonal allergies.
Objective:
Vital signs: blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.

Physical exam: diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally, trachea midline, neck is supple, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruit, strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing on fingertips, brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has moderate intermittent asthma, and her symptoms are consistent with this diagnosis.
Problem list: Asthma; moderate intermittent asthma; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for her albuterol inhaler.
She will continue to use her albuterol inhaler, two puffs every four to six hours as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
Follow-up:
I would like to see the patient again in a month or sooner if her symptoms worsen.
